explanation: Whenever prop_1 becomes true, prop_0 must be true at the same time. This is typically reflected as 'globally, if prop_1 then prop_0'.
This specification means that whenever prop_1 is true, the system shall ensure that prop_0 is also true at that moment.
Whenever prop_1 becomes true, then prop_0 must also be true at that moment. In LTL, this is often represented as globally (if prop_1 then prop_0): G (prop_1 -> prop_0).
Whenever prop_1 holds, prop_0 must also hold at that moment. This is best captured by the formula that globally, prop_1 implies prop_0.

This requirement means that whenever prop_1 is true (i.e., at any moment when prop_1 holds), then prop_0 must hold at that same moment. This is a safety requirement, specifying that prop_0 is required wherever prop_1 occurs.
Whenever prop_1 occurs, prop_0 must also hold at the same time. This is usually interpreted as 'if prop_1, then prop_0' globally.

This requirement means that whenever prop_1 holds, prop_0 should also hold at the same time (in the same state in the temporal model). This is expressed as globally (if prop_1 then prop_0), using the G (globally) operator in LTL.
This means that whenever prop_1 is true, prop_0 must also be true at that time. In LTL, this is usually represented as: globally, if prop_1 holds, then prop_0 must hold.

This requirement says that whenever prop_1 holds (is true), then prop_0 must also hold at that instant. This is an implication that must be true at all times (globally): if prop_1 is true, then prop_0 must also be true.
The requirement states that whenever prop_1 is true, the system shall do prop_0. In LTL, this is expressed as: globally, if prop_1 holds, then prop_0 must also hold.
